title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
source_tags: strategy; growth; design; newsletter; go-to-market; startups
primary_topic: Marketplaces, supply-demand dynamics, and liquidity
secondary_topics: Growth loops, acquisition, virality, referrals, and distribution; B2B GTM, sales, PLG, and enterprise expansion; Founder, fundraising, investing, and company-building lessons; Product strategy, prioritization, roadmaps, and decision-making
specific_topics: product-market fit; positioning and segmentation; strategy and moats; enterprise sales/GTM; SEO/content growth; marketplace liquidity; design/product craft; management and feedback
workflow_use: Apply a tactical playbook; Make or clarify a strategic decision; Diagnose a product or growth problem; Learn from an example or case study
representative_sections_or_prompts: Step 4: Drive initial demand 👋 | 1. Word of mouth | For me, one of the most fascinating learnings from this phase of the research was how impactful word-of-mouth was for early growth of most of today’s biggest marketplace businesses — it was the most important growth channel for over half of the companies. Though this isn’t actually a growth “lever”, it was an enormous growth driver for these companies, and was a strong early signal of Product/Market Fit. Pro tip: If you don’t know what share of your growth is coming from word-of-mouth, you should find out. | 2. Supply driving demand | Just as interesting as the prevalence of word of mouth is how many companies DIDN’T have strong word of mouth early on and still succeeded (also about half). In those cases, companies found other channels to drive demand. | The second most common growth lever (effective for over 40% of marketplaces, and also a big surprise to me) was the marketplaces’ supply directly driving its demand. This is the primary reason these companies focus almost exclusively on supply growth — the supply created the demand. | 3. SEO | SEO, unlike on the supply side where it was only impactful for one company, was a vital early demand growth driver for over 40% of marketplaces. | 4. Performance marketing | The next most common early demand growth lever, instrumental for about a third of marketplaces, was performance marketing — Google, Facebook, and Twitter ads.

title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply

primary_topic: Marketplaces, supply-demand dynamics, and liquidity

representative_sections_or_prompts: Step 3: Drive initial supply 🐥 | 1. Direct sales | One of the most significant learnings (and surprises) for me in doing this research was how important one-on-one direct sales was to most early marketplaces. Sales ended up being a crucial lever for about 60% of the companies I talked to — twice as common as the next biggest lever (piggy-backing and referrals). | 2. Referrals | The second most common early supply-growth lever (tied with the next lever) was offering a referrals program — incentivizing existing supply to refer new supply. About a third of the marketplaces I spoke with saw a lot of success here. | 3. Piggy-back off of an existing network (mostly Craigslist) | About a third of the marketplaces also relied heavily on piggy-backing off of an existing network (in almost every case, Craiglist) to bootstrap their supply growth. I don’t have a lot of quotes here (sorry!), but when it worked, it was the first or second most important lever for these companies. | 4. Word of mouth | Though not really a growth lever, organic word of mouth was a significant factor in the growth of early supply for about a fourth of today’s biggest marketplaces. | 5. Subsidizing
